vulnerability.title: Enable Dracut FIPS Module
vulnerability.description: Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 has an installation-time kernel flag that can enable FIPS mode. The installer must be booted with fips=1 for the system to have FIPS mode enabled. Enabling FIPS mode on a preexisting system is not supported. If this rule fails on an installed system, then this is a permanent finding and cannot be fixed. To enable FIPS, the system requires that the fips module is added in dracut configuration. Check if /etc/dracut.conf.d/40-fips.conf contain add_dracutmodules+=" fips "
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Enable Dracut FIPS Module

triage.reason: Enabling FIPS mode on a pre‑existing RHEL 9 system requires bootloader/kernel changes that are not supported; the rule states the finding is permanent and cannot be fixed. Modifying FIPS settings risks system boot failure and loss of authentication services.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Bootloader/kernel modification; Potential loss of SSH/console access; System boot failure

vulnerability.title: Enable FIPS Mode
vulnerability.description: Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 has an installation-time kernel flag that can enable FIPS mode. The installer must be booted with fips=1 for the system to have FIPS mode enabled. Enabling FIPS mode on a preexisting system is not supported. If this rule fails on an installed system, then this is a permanent finding and cannot be fixed. To enable FIPS mode at bootable container build time configure fips=1 kernel argument in /usr/lib/bootc/kargs.d/01-fips.toml: kargs = ["fips=1"] Then set the cryptographic policy to : update-crypto-policies --no-reload --set
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if ( ! ( [ "${container:-}" == "bwrap-osbuild" ] ) && rpm --quiet -q kernel-core ); then if { rpm --quiet -q kernel rpm-ostree bootc && ! rpm --quiet -q openshift-kubelet && { [ -f "/run/.containerenv" ] || [ -f "/.containerenv" ]; }; }; then cat > /usr/lib/bootc/kargs.d/01-fips.toml << EOF kargs = ["fips=1"] EOF fi else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage.reason: Enabling FIPS mode requires a kernel boot parameter that is only supported at installation or container build time; it cannot be safely applied to an existing system without a full reinstall, posing a high risk of system breakage and loss of functionality.
triage.estimated_impact: reboot required; FIPS mode change; kernel boot parameter; unsupported on existing system; potential loss of functionality

vulnerability.title: Set kernel parameter 'crypto.fips_enabled' to 1
vulnerability.description: System running in FIPS mode is indicated by kernel parameter 'crypto.fips_enabled'. This parameter should be set to 1 in FIPS mode. Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 has an installation-time kernel flag that can enable FIPS mode. The installer must be booted with fips=1 for the system to have FIPS mode enabled. Enabling FIPS mode on a preexisting system is not supported. If this rule fails on an installed system, then this is a permanent finding and cannot be fixed. To enable strict FIPS compliance, the fips=1 kernel option needs to be added to the kernel boot parameters during system installation so key generation is done with FIPS-approved algorithms and continuous monitoring tests in place.
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Set kernel parameter 'crypto.fips_enabled' to 1

triage.reason: Enabling FIPS mode requires adding the fips=1 kernel boot parameter at installation time; it cannot be safely applied to a running system and would require a system reinstall or bootloader reconfiguration, which is beyond a simple remediation and poses a high risk of system breakage.
triage.estimated_impact: reboot required; FIPS mode activation; bootloader configuration; potential system instability

vulnerability.title: The Installed Operating System Is Vendor Supported
vulnerability.description: The installed operating system must be maintained by a vendor. Red Hat Enterprise Linux is supported by Red Hat, Inc. As the Red Hat Enterprise Linux vendor, Red Hat, Inc. is responsible for providing security patches.
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: The Installed Operating System Is Vendor Supported

triage.reason: The finding requires ensuring the OS is a vendor-supported release, which typically involves a standard OS upgrade or patching. This is a routine change that does not involve partitioning, bootloader, or FIPS modifications and is unlikely to cause lockout or critical service breakage if performed correctly.
triage.estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; networking affected; Potential service interruption during OS upgrade; Requires system reboot

vulnerability.title: Ensure /var/tmp Located On Separate Partition
vulnerability.description: The /var/tmp directory is a world-writable directory used for temporary file storage. Ensure it has its own partition or logical volume at installation time, or migrate it using LVM.

triage.reason: Filesystem/partition/mount-option changes can break boot or services and should not be auto-remediated.
triage.estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; filesystems/partitioning; service disruption risk

vulnerability.title: Ensure Red Hat GPG Key Installed
vulnerability.description: To ensure the system can cryptographically verify base software packages come from Red Hat (and to connect to the Red Hat Network to receive them), the Red Hat GPG key must properly be installed. To install the Red Hat GPG key, run: $ sudo subscription-manager register If the system is not connected to the Internet or an RHN Satellite, then install the Red Hat GPG key from trusted media such as the Red Hat installation CD-ROM or DVD. Assuming the disc is mounted in /media/cdrom, use the following command as the root user to import it into the keyring: $ sudo rpm --import /media/cdrom/RPM-GPG-KEY Alternatively, the key may be pre-loaded during the RHEL installation. In such cases, the key can be installed by running the following command: sudo rpm --import /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # The two fingerprints below are retrieved from https://access.redhat.com/security/team/key readonly REDHAT_RELEASE_FINGERPRINT="567E347AD0044ADE55BA8A5F199E2F91FD431D51" readonly REDHAT_AUXILIARY_FINGERPRINT="7E4624258C406535D56D6F135054E4A45A6340B3" # Location of the key we would like to import (once it's integrity verified) readonly REDHAT_RELEASE_KEY="/etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release" RPM_GPG_DIR_PERMS=$(stat -c %a "$(dirname "$REDHAT_RELEASE_KEY")") # Verify /etc/pki/rpm-gpg directory permissions are safe if [ "${RPM_GPG_DIR_PERMS}" -le "755" ] then # If they are safe, try to obtain fingerprints from the key file # (to ensure there won't be e.g. CRC error). readarray -t GPG_OUT < <(gpg --show-keys --with-fingerprint --with-colons "$REDHAT_RELEASE_KEY" | grep -A1 "^pub" | gr

triage.reason: Importing the Red Hat GPG key is a standard, non‑disruptive operation that does not affect authentication, networking, or filesystem integrity. It follows the STIG hardening pattern for package key installation and carries no risk of lockout or service breakage.

commands_executed: curl -s https://www.redhat.com/security/team/key | sed -n "/-----BEGIN PGP PUBLIC KEY BLOCK-----/,/-----END PGP PUBLIC KEY BLOCK-----/p" > /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release

llm_verdict.message:
**Remediation status**

1. `/etc/pki/rpm-gpg` has been created with the correct permissions.  
2. A recent attempt to download the Red Hat GPG key was made, but the key file that was written was empty – the key block was not extracted from the HTML page at `https://www.redhat.com/security/team/key`.  
3. Consequently, the `rpm --import` command failed with exit code 1 because the key file does not contain a valid ASCII‑armored key.

**Next steps**

Please run the following command to fetch the key block correctly and import it:

```bash
# Download only the key block from the Red Hat key page and write it to the key file
curl -s https://www.redhat.com/security/team/key | grep -A 1000 "-----BEGIN PGP PUBLIC KEY BLOCK-----" > /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release

# Verify the key contains a valid block
file /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release

# Import the key into RPM’s keyring
rpm --import /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release
```

Confirm that the import succeeds (you should see a message like “key <fingerprint> imported”) and then re‑run the OpenSCAP scan. If the key cannot be retrieved from the URL, consider obtaining it from a trusted local media or your Red Hat customer portal.

per_turn.command: curl -s https://www.redhat.com/security/team/key | sed -n "/-----BEGIN PGP PUBLIC KEY BLOCK-----/,/-----END PGP PUBLIC KEY BLOCK-----/p" > /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release

review_verdict.feedback: The plan will install the Red Hat GPG key into the RPM keyring, thereby satisfying the compliance requirement. However, it lacks critical security checks such as fingerprint verification and should be enhanced with additional safety steps to avoid installing a compromised key. The approach is functional but not fully optimal.
concerns: No explicit fingerprint verification against the known Red Hat fingerprint before importing the key
No check for pre‑existing RPM key to avoid unnecessary import
No verification that the key file was successfully downloaded and is non‑empty
Depends on an external keyserver that may not be trusted or available in isolated environments
Missing error handling or idempotence logic for the import steps
suggested_improvements: Add a step to compute the fingerprint of the downloaded key using `gpg --with-fingerprint` or `rpm --checksig --import` and compare it to the documented fingerprint (567E347AD0044ADE55BA8A5F199E2F91FD431D51) before importing.
Check `rpm -q gpg-pubkey | grep 0fd431d5` before fetching the key to skip if already present.
Verify that the downloaded key file is non‑empty and valid before moving it to /etc/pki/rpm-gpg.
Implement basic error handling: exit if any of the key fetch/export/import steps fail.
Prefer using Red Hat's own keyserver or CDN (or `subscription-manager register`) instead of arbitrary keyservers.
Set the key file permissions to 0644 and verify `/etc/pki/rpm-gpg` directory is 0755 to comply with hardening standards.

Risk of installing malicious packages due to incorrect key

vulnerability.title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Enforce for root User
vulnerability.description: The pam_pwquality module's enforce_for_root parameter controls requirements for enforcing password complexity for the root user. Enable the enforce_for_root setting in /etc/security/pwquality.conf to require the root user to use complex passwords.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q libpwquality; }; then if [ -e "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" ] ; then LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*enforce_for_root/Id" "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" else touch "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" fi # make sure file has newline at the end sed -i -e '$a\' "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" cp "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" "/etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak" # Insert at the end of the file printf '%s\n' "enforce_for_root" >> "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" # Clean up after ourselves. rm "/etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak" else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage.reason: Enabling enforce_for_root in pam_pwquality.conf is a standard PAM configuration change that follows STIG hardening patterns and does not affect critical services or system boot. It is a simple file edit with minimal risk of lockout if the root password already meets complexity requirements.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential root password complexity enforcement may affect root login if password is weak

The OpenSCAP rule **Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements – Enforce for root User** has been remediated on Rocky Linux 10.

### Key remediation steps performed

1. **Backed up** the existing `/etc/security/pwquality.conf` by renaming it to `pwquality.conf.bak`.  
2. **Re‑created** the original file from the backup.  
3. **Removed** any pre‑existing `enforce_for_root` configuration line using an anchored `sed` command.  
4. **Appended** a single, explicit `enforce_for_root=1` line to enforce complex password requirements for the root user.  
5. **Ensured** the configuration file contains a terminating newline.

### Result
The file now contains exactly one `enforce_for_root=1` directive and all other settings remain unchanged. This activates the requirement that root password changes meet the system‑wide password quality policy.

You can verify the change with:

```bash
grep -i '^enforce_for_root' /etc/security/pwquality.conf
```

The OpenSCAP scanner should now report the finding as **passed**.

llm_verdict.message:
The OpenSCAP rule **Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements – Enforce for root User** has been remediated on Rocky Linux 10.

### Key remediation steps performed

1. **Backed up** the existing `/etc/security/pwquality.conf` by renaming it to `pwquality.conf.bak`.  
2. **Re‑created** the original file from the backup.  
3. **Removed** any pre‑existing `enforce_for_root` configuration line using an anchored `sed` command.  
4. **Appended** a single, explicit `enforce_for_root=1` line to enforce complex password requirements for the root user.  
5. **Ensured** the configuration file contains a terminating newline.

### Result
The file now contains exactly one `enforce_for_root=1` directive and all other settings remain unchanged. This activates the requirement that root password changes meet the system‑wide password quality policy.

You can verify the change with:

```bash
grep -i '^enforce_for_root' /etc/security/pwquality.conf
```

The OpenSCAP scanner should now report the finding as **passed**.

review_verdict.feedback: The plan correctly enforces the enforce_for_root setting, handles backups, anchors patterns, and avoids duplicate entries. It satisfies the vulnerability remediation requirements with minimal risk. Minor refinements are suggested for safety and clarity.
concerns: Potential administrator lockout if the existing root password does not meet the new complexity rules, as the plan does not proactively verify the password policy before enabling it.

The backup is created via renaming, which could temporarily remove the file from its original location; this is acceptable but may be surprising if further processes expect the file to exist during the backup period.
The script does not automatically enforce restoration of original file permissions and ownership after editing; though the backup retains them, a new copy might inherit different defaults.
suggested_improvements:
Add a pre‑remediation check that the current root password satisfies minimal complexity to avoid lockout. If not, prompt the admin or refuse to apply the change.

Incorporate a post‑remediation test by attempting a root password change or by using pwquality to validate enforcement, and abort if enforcement fails.

Restore the original permissions and ownership on /etc/security/pwquality.conf after modifications, or explicitly set them (e.g., chmod 644, chown root:root).

Provide an automated rollback script that copies the .bak file back if the change needs to be reverted, possibly triggered by failure of the test step.

Simplify by removing the delete-and-then-grep pattern: a single sed can replace or append enforce_for_root=1 when absent.

Ensure the script exits with a non‑zero status on any error to avoid silent failures.

qa_result.verdict_reason: The plan correctly implements the enforce_for_root setting, backs up the original configuration, and uses anchored patterns to avoid unintended edits.  PAM password policies affect only password changes; they do not interrupt running services such as sshd, auditd, firewalld, or networking.  The only expected side effect is that future root password changes will require compliance with the new complexity policy, which is inherent to the remediation and does not lock out existing services.
side_effects: Future root password changes must satisfy the new complexity requirements; attempting to change the root password may fail if it does not meet the policy.
No impact on SSH key‑based login or other authentication mechanisms.

vulnerability.title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Length
vulnerability.description: The pam_pwquality module's minlen parameter controls requirements for minimum characters required in a password. Add minlen= after pam_pwquality to set minimum password length requirements.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q libpwquality; }; then var_password_pam_minlen='' if grep -sq minlen /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf ; then sed -i "/minlen/d" /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf fi # Strip any search characters in the key arg so that the key can be replaced without # adding any search characters to the config file. stripped_key=$(sed 's/[\^=\$,;+]*//g' <<< "^minlen") # shellcheck disable=SC2059 printf -v formatted_output "%s = %s" "$stripped_key" "$var_password_pam_minlen" # If the key exists, change it. Otherwise, add it to the config_file. # We search for the key string followed by a word boundary (matched by \>), # so if we search for 'setting', 'setting2' won't match. if LC_ALL=C grep -q -m

triage.reason: Standard STIG password policy change that does not affect critical services or system boot; typical remediation is a simple config edit.

vulnerability.title: Only Authorized Local User Accounts Exist on Operating System
vulnerability.description: Enterprise Application tends to use the server or virtual machine exclusively. Besides the default operating system user, there should be only authorized local users required by the installed software groups and applications that exist on the operating system. The authorized user list can be customized in the refine value variable var_accounts_authorized_local_users_regex. OVAL regular expression is used for the user list. Configure the system so all accounts on the system are assigned to an active system, application, or user account. Remove accounts that do not support approved system activities or that allow for a normal user to perform administrative-level actions. To remove unauthorized system accounts, use the following command: $ sudo userdel unauthorized_user
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Only Authorized Local User Accounts Exist on Operating System

vulnerability.title: Set the Boot Loader Admin Username to a Non-Default Value
vulnerability.description: The grub2 boot loader should have a superuser account and password protection enabled to protect boot-time settings. To maximize the protection, select a password-protected superuser account with unique name, and modify the /etc/grub.d/01_users configuration file to reflect the account name change. Do not to use common administrator account names like root, admin, or administrator for the grub2 superuser account. Change the superuser to a different username (The default is 'root'). $ sed -i 's/\(set superusers=\).*/\1"<unique user ID>"/g' /etc/grub.d/01_users The line mentioned above must be followed by the line export superusers so that the superusers is honored. Once the superuser account has been added, update the grub.cfg file by running: grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Set the Boot Loader Admin Username to a Non-Default Value

triage.reason: The change involves modifying the GRUB2 boot loader configuration, which is a critical system component. Incorrect configuration can render the system unbootable or lock out all console/SSH access. According to policy, bootloader changes are classified as too dangerous to remediate without human review.

vulnerability.title: Configure Multiple DNS Servers in /etc/resolv.conf
vulnerability.description: Determine whether the system is using local or DNS name resolution with the following command: $ sudo grep hosts /etc/nsswitch.conf hosts: files dns If the DNS entry is missing from the host's line in the "/etc/nsswitch.conf" file, the "/etc/resolv.conf" file must be empty. Verify the "/etc/resolv.conf" file is empty with the following command: $ sudo ls -al /etc/resolv.conf -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 0 Aug 19 08:31 resolv.conf If the DNS entry is found on the host's line of the "/etc/nsswitch.conf" file, then verify the following: Multiple Domain Name System (DNS) Servers should be configured in /etc/resolv.conf. This provides redundant name resolution services in the event that a domain server crashes. To configure the system to contain as least 2 DNS servers, add a corresponding nameserver ip_address entry in /etc/resolv.conf for each DNS server where ip_address is the IP address of a valid DNS server. For example: search example.com nameserver 192.168.0.1 nameserver 192.168.0.2
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Configure Multiple DNS Servers in /etc/resolv.conf

triage.reason: Adding a second DNS server to /etc/resolv.conf is a standard, low-risk configuration change that does not affect authentication, authorization, or critical services. It follows typical STIG hardening patterns and is unlikely to cause lockout or system breakage.
triage.estimated_impact: networking affected; Incorrect DNS entry may lead to name resolution failures

review_verdict.feedback: The plan would functionally fix the vulnerability by ensuring /etc/resolv.conf contains at least two nameserver entries, or is empty when DNS is disabled. However it has several areas that could be improved to align with best practices and avoid potential issues.
concerns: May overwrite /etc/resolv.conf even when it is a symlink managed by systemd‑resolved or NetworkManager, causing name resolution problems.
Hard‑coded DNS servers (e.g., public DNS) may not honor enterprise DNS policy or local internal name resolution.
Does not preserve existing search or domain directives, potentially breaking application expectations.
File permissions and ownership may be lost if the file is rewritten without care.
No validation that the provided IP addresses are reachable or authoritative DNS servers.
suggested_improvements: Check if /etc/resolv.conf is a symlink and, if so, use systemd-resolve or NetworkManager configuration instead of writing the file directly.
Detect existing local DNS servers from the network manager or DHCP and prefer them over public DNS unless policy dictates otherwise.
Preserve search and domain lines by appending nameserver entries rather than truncating the file.
Ensure file ownership remains root:root and mode 644 after modifications.
Validate the DNS server IPs with ping or dig before adding them to prevent broken resolution.

qa_result.verdict_reason: The proposed remediation correctly implements the requirement to have at least two nameserver entries (or an empty file when DNS is disabled) and addresses the core vulnerability. The implementation is unlikely to disrupt critical services such as sshd, auditd, or firewalld in normal operation. However, there are potential side effects related to systems that use NetworkManager or systemd-resolved to manage /etc/resolv.conf and to environments where hard‑coded DNS addresses may bypass internal naming resolve policies. These risks are manageable and can be mitigated by testing on a non‑production node first.
side_effects: May overwrite a symlinked /etc/resolv.conf managed by NetworkManager/systemd-resolved, which could temporarily disrupt DNS resolution until the manager re‑writes the file.
Replacing the file may drop existing search/domain directives, affecting applications that rely on them.
Using hard‑coded public DNS servers can bypass enterprise DNS policy or internal namespace resolution.
File permissions and ownership may be reset if the file is rewritten without preserving them.

vulnerability.title: Prevent Unrestricted Mail Relaying
vulnerability.description: Modify the /etc/postfix/main.cf file to restrict client connections to the local network with the following command: $ sudo postconf -e 'smtpd_client_restrictions = permit_mynetworks,reject'
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && rpm --quiet -q postfix; then if ! grep -q ^smtpd_client_restrictions /etc/postfix/main.cf; then echo "smtpd_client_restrictions = permit_mynetworks,reject" >> /etc/postfix/main.cf else sed -i "s/^smtpd_client_restrictions.*/smtpd_client_restrictions = permit_mynetworks,reject/g" /etc/postfix/main.cf fi else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage.reason: The remediation involves a straightforward configuration change to /etc/postfix/main.cf that limits client connections to the local network. This is a standard STIG hardening step for a non-critical service and does not risk lockout or system breakage.
triage.estimated_impact: Potential temporary mail relay disruption if misconfigured; Requires postfix restart to apply changes

vulnerability.title: Configure Time Service Maxpoll Interval
vulnerability.description: The maxpoll should be configured to in /etc/ntp.conf or /etc/chrony.conf (or /etc/chrony.d/) to continuously poll time servers. To configure maxpoll in /etc/ntp.conf or /etc/chrony.conf (or /etc/chrony.d/) add the following after each server, pool or peer entry: maxpoll to server directives. If using chrony, any pool directives should be configured too.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { ( rpm --quiet -q chrony || rpm --quiet -q ntp ); }; then var_time_service_set_maxpoll='' pof="/usr/sbin/pidof" CONFIG_FILES="/etc/ntp.conf" $pof ntpd || { CHRONY_D_PATH=/etc/chrony.d/ mapfile -t CONFIG_FILES < <(find ${CHRONY_D_PATH}.* -type f -name '*.conf') CONFIG_FILES+=(/etc/chrony.conf) } # get list of ntp files for config_file in "${CONFIG_FILES[@]}" ; do # Set maxpoll values to var_time_service_set_maxpoll sed -i "s/^\(\(server\|pool\|peer\).*maxpoll\) [0-9,-][0-9]*\(.*\)$/\1 $var_time_service_set_maxpoll \3/" "$config_file" done for config_file in "${CONFIG_FILES[@]}" ; do # Add maxpoll to server, pool or peer entries without maxpoll grep "^\(server\|pool\|peer\)" "$config_file" | grep -v maxpo

triage.reason: The remediation involves editing a standard configuration file for the NTP/chrony service to set the maxpoll interval, a routine change that does not affect authentication, critical services, or system boot. It is unlikely to cause lockout or system breakage.
triage.estimated_impact: networking affected; Incorrect maxpoll value could lead to time drift or increased network traffic

# Use NTP servers from DHCP.
sourcedir /run/chrony-dhcp

# Record the rate at which the system clock gains/losses time.
driftfile /var/lib/chrony/drift

# Allow the system clock to be stepped in the first three updates
# if its offset is larger than 1 second.

# Enable kernel synchronization of the real-time clock (RTC).

# Insert/delete leap seconds by slewing instead of stepping.

active

review_verdict.feedback: The proposed plan correctly identifies all relevant NTP/chrony configuration files, backs them up, updates server/pool/peer directives to use a maxpoll value of 10, verifies the configuration syntax, and reloads the appropriate daemon. This will resolve the vulnerability if executed correctly.
concerns: Ensuring that `systemctl reload ntpd` is supported on all target distributions (some may require a restart instead),
The regex logic must reliably detect lines that are half-commented or contain inline comments; mismatch could leave some directives unchanged
The plan assumes the executables `chronyd` and `ntpd` support the `-t` test option; on older versions this may differ
Potential edge cases where configuration files include custom options (e.g., server +foo) that could interfere with the insertion logic
suggested_improvements: Add a verification step that the daemon actually reloads successfully and continues to time‑sync after reloading
Explicitly limit the maximum allowed maxpoll value to the supported range (e.g., 6–16) instead of hardcoding 10
Include checks for whether the system uses a systemd service name that differs from `chronyd`/`ntpd`, or use `systemctl list-unit-files | grep -i ntp` to discover the correct unit
Consider a more robust pattern that ignores lines that are commented out after the directive (e.g., `server foo # comment` that also has a trailing `#` comment string)

qa_result.verdict_reason: The remediation correctly backs up configuration files, updates only NTP/chrony server, pool, and peer directives to set maxpoll to 10, tests the configuration before reloading, and reverts if a syntax error is detected. The change only affects the NTP/chrony service; a reload has no impact on SSH, auditd, firewalld, or networking, and the backup/revert procedure prevents permanent misconfiguration.
side_effects: A brief, non-disruptive reload of the chronyd or ntpd daemon, which may temporarily delay time synchronization. No other services are impacted.

vulnerability.title: Enable Certmap in SSSD
vulnerability.description: SSSD should be configured to verify the certificate of the user or group. To set this up ensure that section like certmap/testing.test/rule_name is setup in /etc/sssd/sssd.conf. For example [certmap/testing.test/rule_name] matchrule =<SAN>.*EDIPI@mil maprule = (userCertificate;binary={cert!bin}) domains = testing.test
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Enable Certmap in SSSD

triage.reason: Enabling certmap in SSSD is a standard authentication configuration change that follows STIG hardening patterns and is unlikely to cause lockout or system breakage.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Incorrect certmap configuration may result in authentication failures for users or groups
